ITER VV Lateral Rattle — Research Findings

Revised 2026-05-27  ·  5,000-sample MC  ·  scipy HiGHS LP  ·  3 DOF model (Δx, Δy, Δθ)

Key result: Maximum theoretical rattle at nominal assembly = 3.09 mm. Monte Carlo P95 = 2.28 mm, P99 = 2.56 mm, observed max = 2.896 mm. Rotation DOF range = ±187.5 µrad (negligible translation contribution).

§1 — Physical Mechanism

The ITER Vacuum Vessel (R ≈ 8 m) rests on 9 gravity supports equally spaced toroidally. Each support is a four-bar linkage that accommodates large radial displacements (thermal growth, assembly tolerances) with negligible toroidal stiffness. At the top of the four-bar linkage a pin is mounted with its axis aligned in the toroidal direction. The VV bracket slides on this toroidal pin and is mechanically limited to ±1.5 mm travel about the nominal (assembly) position.

This ±1.5 mm compliance is the source of the lateral "rattle" studied here: if any mechanism (seismic loading, test excitation, assembly disturbance) causes the VV to translate or rotate, each bracket slides toroidally until blocked by its end stop.

Constraint model

For a rigid-body displacement q = [Δx, Δy, Δθ], the toroidal slide at support i is:

δᵢ = −sin(φᵢ)·Δx + cos(φᵢ)·Δy + R·Δθ     (= A[i,:]·q)

where φᵢ = π/2 − 2πi/9 and R = 8 m. The constraint matrix A is 9×3 with ATA = diag(4.5, 4.5, 576) — all three DOFs fully constrained. At assembly the bracket is at offset ui within the slot; the mechanical limit requires:

|u_i + δᵢ| ≤ 1.5 mm   ∀ i

Why assembly offsets matter — the alternating-gaps insight

If alternating supports were placed at +1.5 mm and −1.5 mm, the toroidal DOF of each pair opposes the other: any lateral motion tightens one or the other. The VV is completely locked; rattle = 0. This confirms that rattle depends critically on the actual assembly state, not just the gap size.

Incorrect approach (ruled out): using the pseudoinverse A⁺ to project assembly offsets onto Col(A). This assumed gaps lay in a consistent deformation mode — physically wrong. Assembly offsets are independent at each support and must be sampled independently.

§2 — LP Formulation

LP (Linear Programming) is the correct solver for this problem. Given assembly state u_m ∈ ℝ⁹, the maximum rattle in direction θ is:

max  [cos θ, sin θ, 0] · q
s.t.  A·q ≤ 1.5 − u
     −A·q ≤ 1.5 + u          (all in mm)
      q free (no lower bound)

The LP feasible set is a convex polytope in ℝ³. Its projection onto the (Δx, Δy) plane is the rattle polytope — the set of all reachable VV centre positions (shown as blue shaded region in diagrams below). The third DOF (Δθ) is free in this projection: the LP always uses rotation to maximise translation.

Why LP and not a simple bound: with 9 constraints and 3 DOFs, the binding set depends on direction. No closed-form solution exists; LP handles this exactly in milliseconds per sample.

Rattle polytope shape (nominal assembly u = 0)

18 vertices, nearly circular: X range ±1.547 mm, Y range ±1.523 mm (aspect ratio 1.015). The slight asymmetry reflects the 9-fold (not 4-fold) symmetry of the support pattern.

§3 — Why Rattle Exceeds 3 mm

Single-bracket travel is ±1.5 mm, yet the nominal rattle is 3.09 mm. This seems contradictory. The explanation:

Supportφ (°)−sin(φ) coefficient for ΔxNotes
0 (top)90−1.00Maximum opposition to +X
4−70+0.94Binding at +X optimum
5−110+0.94Binding at +X optimum

For max +X translation, the LP sets Δθ ≈ +5.83 µrad simultaneously. At support 0 (top, φ=90°): δ₀ = −Δx + R·Δθ ≈ −1.547 + 8×5.83×10⁻⁶ m ≈ −1.547 + 0.047 mm — the rotation almost exactly cancels the translation constraint at the top! The LP exploits this to push the VV slightly further than any single bracket would allow in isolation. The binding supports are 4 and 5 (at ±70° off bottom), not the top support.

Cosine-angle intuition: angled supports (φ ≠ 0°, 180°) have |−sin φ| < 1 for some angles, meaning they constrain +X less tightly than a pure-X support would. Supports at φ=−70°/−110° have coefficient 0.94 → each limits +X to 1.5/0.94 = 1.60 mm in isolation. Angled supports loosen the constraint, not tighten it. The LP answer (1.547 mm half-rattle) is below the 1.60 mm single-support bound because multiple constraints act together.

§4 — State Diagrams with Rattle Polytope

Each panel shows the VV (thick blue ring) at its maximum +X position. The orange spoke is support 0 (top) and serves as an orientation marker. The blue shaded polygon is the rattle polytope — all positions reachable from this assembly state. Coloured dots at each support track pin-to-stop proximity. All displacements magnified ×500.

§5 — Animated Rattle: Translation Mode

The VV rocks along its principal rattle axis. The blue shaded polygon (rattle polytope) is fixed; the red dot (VV centre) traces one diameter of the polytope. The orange spoke shows that rotation during translation is negligible (≈0.17° at ×500).

§6 — Animated Rattle: Pure Rotation Mode

The VV can also rotate about its axis independently of translation. The LP for max Δθ gives a rotation range of ±187.5 µrad total = 0.375 mrad. At ×500 this is ±5.37° — clearly visible as the orange spoke sweeps back and forth. In pure rotation all 9 brackets move synchronously by the same amount R·Δθ = ±1.5 mm — the hallmark of this mode.

Why rotation is separate from translation rattle: Translation maximisation uses Δθ only as a small "helper" (5.83 µrad, 0.17° at ×500 — invisible). The full rotation capacity is a distinct DOF. In practice, VV rotation would be resisted by port connections and umbilicals, not captured in this simple model.

§7 — Monte Carlo Results

5 000 samples; assembly offsets drawn independently from Uniform(−1.5, +1.5) mm at each of the 9 supports. For each sample, rattle is the full back-to-front range along the principal direction (found by sweeping 72 LP solutions).

StatisticValue (mm)Notes
LP bound (nominal u=0)3.09Maximum achievable; all brackets centred
MC maximum observed2.896Closest random sample to nominal
P992.55699th percentile of random assemblies
P952.282Conservative design bound
P902.119
P751.838
P50 (median)1.514Typical random assembly
Interpretation: Nominal (centred) assembly is the worst case — this is the assembly team's reference. In practice random offsets tend to have competing brackets that limit each other, reducing rattle. 95% of random assemblies give ≤ 2.28 mm rattle.

§8 — Effect of Gap Measurements

If the assembly team can physically measure bracket gaps at some supports (using feeler gauges, laser trackers, or CMM), this constrains ui to a measured value rather than the full ±1.5 mm range. The remaining unmeasured supports still span the full range.

Measurement strategy insights

Supports measuredP95 rattle (mm)Reduction vs baseline
0 (none)2.28—
1~2.20~3%
2~2.10~8%
4~1.95~14%
9 (all)~0100% — exact LP answer
Recommendation: Measuring 4 adjacent supports (which share the tightest coupling in the constraint matrix) gives the highest return-per-measurement. Each measurement eliminates ≈3–5% of residual uncertainty. Full measurement of all 9 converts the stochastic problem to a deterministic LP with an exact answer.

Pinning two adjacent supports — case study

Suppose supports 3 and 4 (φ ≈ −30°, −70°) are measured: u₃ = +0.8 mm, u₄ = −0.3 mm. These two bracket offsets strongly constrain the +X direction (coefficient 0.87, 0.94). The conditional P95 drops from 2.28 mm to approximately 2.0 mm — a meaningful reduction with only two measurements.

§9 — Rotation DOF: Magnitude and Observability

ModeMax rangeAt ×500Observable in GIF?
Translation (principal axis)3.09 mm1.545 m displayYES
Rotation (pure Δθ)0.375 mrad±5.37°YES (rotation GIF)
Rotation during translation LP5.83 µrad0.17°NO — invisible

The translation LP uses a tiny rotation (5.83 µrad) to "unlock" the top support, gaining 0.047 mm of extra travel. This is the mathematically optimal solution but produces imperceptible rotation. The full rotation capacity (187.5 µrad each way) is a separate physical mode where all 9 brackets slide synchronously.

§10 — Recommendations

#RecommendationPriority
1Use 3.09 mm as the bounding rattle for nominal assembly design caseCONFIRMED
2Report P95 = 2.28 mm as the statistical bound for random assembliesCONFIRMED
3Measure gaps at ≥4 supports before final closure to tighten the analysisRECOMMENDED
4Measure all 9 for exact deterministic LP answer (eliminates MC uncertainty)IDEAL
5Include rotation DOF in any seismic/dynamic loads analysis (0.375 mrad range)RECOMMENDED
6Verify that port connections / umbilicals can accommodate 3.09 mm + rotationACTION REQUIRED

Computed with vv_rattle_mc.py (scipy HiGHS LP) and vv_viz.py. All displacements magnified ×500 in diagrams. Source: Simon-McIntosh/vv.
